def: Free from prejudice; judging without bias.
A country with a lower level of industrial and economic development.
The state of being very poor.
The unfair use of people or resources for one's own gain.
A body that provides help to people in need.
The state of being financially comfortable and well-off.
To support and encourage the growth of something.
To become more severe or serious.
Placed in a worse position than others; lacking fair chances.
The basic laws and principles by which a country is governed.
The process of voting to choose leaders; also a choice.
The group of elected people who make a country's laws.
The political parties not in government; also resistance to something.
The principle that everyone is subject to and protected by the law.
The authority given by voters; also a seat in parliament.
To rule and run a country or region.
The stirring up of strong feelings, often to influence opinion unfairly.
A deep split that sets groups against each other.
A person unfairly blamed for the faults of others.
Aggressive campaigning that stirs up hatred against a group.
A belief that events are secretly caused by a powerful group.
To stir people up to hostility or violence.
To make something seem simpler than it really is.
Causing people to split into strongly opposed groups.
A basic right guaranteed to every person by law.
The right to hold and voice one's own opinions.
A share in the responsibility for something.
The act of opposing or fighting against something.
A formal written request, often signed by many people.
To fight back against attack or unfair treatment.
To speak or act in support of a cause or person.
Able and entitled to make one's own responsible decisions.
The moral principles that guide how a business acts.
Dishonest use of power for private gain, such as bribery.
The duty to explain and answer for one's actions.
A bad or unjust state of affairs that needs to be fixed.
To reveal something hidden, especially wrongdoing.
Honest and morally upright.
A focus on serving the good of society as a whole.
A pledge to act in a certain way, made of one's own accord.
A person or group with an interest in an organisation's affairs.
Making something appear more environmentally friendly than it is.
A summary of finances; also an overall assessment of results.
To fix something firmly so it becomes lasting.
Believable and worthy of trust.
The reorganising of work to make it more efficient; also making excuses.
The loss of a person's employment.
The gaining of new skills or qualifications for work.
The making of arrangements, such as working hours, more flexible.
The dissolving of clear limits, as between work and private life.
To take the place of something, or put something new in its place.
To change the way an organisation or system is arranged.
Insecure and uncertain, especially of work or income.
A form of AI in which systems learn patterns from data.
A computing system loosely modelled on the brain, used in AI.
A collection of related data treated as a unit.
An unfair leaning that distorts judgement or results.
The state of being self-governing and independent.
To improve a skill through practice; also to teach a model with data.
Able to do a great deal of work effectively.
The linking of people or systems into a connected whole.
A point where two systems meet and interact.
Remote servers on the internet used to store data and run software.
The coding of data so only authorised people can read it.
Steady improvement or movement toward a better state.
To make something happen or move faster.
Introducing something entirely new and important.
A statement of what is expected to happen in the future.
The process of growing or being gradually improved.
A clear idea of what the future could or should be like.
The chance that something bad might happen.
A gradual, far-reaching change in something.
To say what will happen before it does.
Able to be expected or predicted.
Pointing the way forward; setting a new direction.
The feeling of being an accepted part of a group.
The underground part of a plant; also the origin of something.
What is passed down from earlier generations or from the dead.
A lasting mark left on someone by early experiences.
A feeling of being cut off from others or from society.
To feel a close connection with someone or something.
Firmly fixed by deep and lasting connections.
The ability to speak two languages fluently.
The switching between languages within a conversation.
A common language used between speakers of different tongues.
A way of pronouncing a language that shows origin; also stress on a syllable.
The story of the languages a person has learned and used.
To move from one thing to another; also to exchange.
Done easily, without visible effort.
The dominant power to define how something is understood.
The pushing of a person or group to the edges of society.
A person or medium used to express the views of others.
An idea or feeling a word suggests beyond its literal meaning.
The act of affecting how someone thinks or behaves.
To have an effect on how something develops or behaves.
To shut a person or group out of society or a group.
Expressing a judgement of good or bad.
An idea suggested as a starting point, to be tested.
The system of methods used in a field of study.
Research aimed at understanding, not at immediate use.
The checking of research by other experts before publication.
A particular area of study.
To support a claim with evidence; also to occupy.
To explain the meaning of something.
Based on observation and experiment rather than theory alone.
Combining knowledge from several fields of study.
The study of how context shapes the meaning of language.
The rules for arranging words into sentences.
A small word like 'doch' or 'mal' that colours the tone of a sentence.
The process by which a person learns a language.
The ability to use several languages.
A fixed phrase where a verb and noun together carry the meaning.
To express something in different words.
Relating to language or the study of language.
The trend toward shaping life around the individual.
The splitting of opinion into two strongly opposed groups.
The groups and citizens active in public life outside the state.
A gradual change in what a society considers important.
The well-being of society as a whole.
Unity and support among people with shared interests.
To be a typical feature of; also to label or mark.
To pick up and pursue an idea or topic.
Relating to the active, organised part of society outside the state.
Concerning what would have happened under different conditions.
Acting in accordance with rules, laws, or standards.
A discussion aimed at agreement; also a court hearing.
A formal statement giving one's view on an issue.
A formal written message or document.
An agreement reached when each side gives up something.
To mention or make reference to something.
